coredata.dc:title: Visible-light-driven hierarchical porous CeO2 derived from wood for effective photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue
coredata.dc:creator: Cheng, Zhuoying
Luo, Suyue
Liu, Zhao
Zhang, Ying
Liao, Yushuang
Guo, Minghui
Nguyen, Tat Thang
coredata.dc:description: To improve the photodegradation efficiency of organic dyes under visible light, a novel bio-inspired photocatalyst was designed herein based on the theory of wood-bionics. Graded porous cerium oxide with wood structure (W–CeO2) was prepared by using larch wood splinters as the template. W–CeO2 maintains the structure of the wood. W–CeO2 showed many advantages such as low consumption, high performance, and long-term stability for application as a photocatalyst. In this study, the morphological features, crystalline structure, specific surface area, chemical composition, carrier separation efficiency and optical properties of the as-developed photocatalyst were investigated comprehensively. The results show that W–CeO2 completely replicated the multi-scale hierarchical pore structure of the wood template. Compared with cerium oxide prepared by the traditional hydrothermal method (0-CeO2), the specific surface area of W–CeO2 was doubled, and the average pore diameter was reduced by 73% to 5.38 nm. Moreover, W–CeO2 showed the characteristics of anisotropy, circular electron diffraction pattern, reduced crystallinity, and higher charge separation efficiency. W–CeO2 also possessed higher electronic activity, greater active oxygen content, and lower electron-hole recombination efficiency. Methylene blue (MB) dye was used as the target pollutant to test the photocatalytic performance of W–CeO2. The MB degradation test results show that using wood as a template greatly improved the catalytic efficiency of the catalyst. The main free radicals involved in the reaction were determined by adding 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ) and tert-butanol (TBA) for comparative experiments. The results of this work can provide new insights into the construction of bio-inspired materials for promoting the photocatalytic reaction of organic dyes and indicate that W–CeO2 photocatalyst is a promising candidate for wastewater treatment application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Wood morphology
Hierarchical porous structure
Charge transfer
Visible-light-driven

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of W–CeO2: (a) cross section, (b) (c) (d) vertical section.
TEM images of 0-CeO2: (a)(b) microstructure of 0-CeO2, (c) HRTEM image of 0-CeO2, (d) SAED pattern of 0-CeO2.
TEM images of W–CeO2: (a), (b), (c), (d) Micro morphology; (e), (f), (g) HRTEM image; (h) SAED pattern.
(a) XRD patterns of 0-CeO2 and W–CeO2, (b) N2 absorption of 0-CeO2 and W–CeO2; (c) Pore volume distribution curve of 0-CeO2 and W–CeO2.
(a) XPS survey of samples; (b) Ce3d fitted curve of 0-CeO2 and W–CeO2; (c) O1s fitted curve of 0-CeO2 and W–CeO2.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of 0-CeO2 and W–CeO2; (b) the plots of [F(R∞)hν]2 vs. hν for 0-CeO2 and W–CeO2.

(a) Photocatalytic degradation of MB by the photocatalysts under visible light, (b) PL spectra of 0-CeO2 and W–CeO2, (c) Cyclic experiment of photocatalytic degradation of MB by W–CeO2 (d) Free radical scavenging experiment.
Schematic diagram of photocatalytic mechanism of W–CeO2.
BET structural parameters of 0-CeO2 and W–CeO2.
Comparison of Ce and O elemental contents.

head.ce:title: Visible-light-driven hierarchical porous CeO2 derived from wood for effective photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science & Technology (Northeast Forestry University), Ministry of Education, Harbin, 150040, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science & Technology (Northeast Forestry University)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science & Technology (Northeast Forestry University), Ministry of Education, Harbin 150040, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Wood Industry and Interior Design, Vietnam National University of Forestry, Xuan Mai, Hanoi, 13417, Viet Nam

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Wood Industry and Interior Design

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Wood Industry and Interior Design, Vietnam National University of Forestry, Xuan Mai, Hanoi 13417, Vietnam

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to the work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To improve the photodegradation efficiency of organic dyes under visible light, a novel bio-inspired photocatalyst was designed herein based on the theory of wood-bionics. Graded porous cerium oxide with wood structure (W–CeO2) was prepared by using larch wood splinters as the template. W–CeO2 maintains the structure of the wood. W–CeO2 showed many advantages such as low consumption, high performance, and long-term stability for application as a photocatalyst. In this study, the morphological features, crystalline structure, specific surface area, chemical composition, carrier separation efficiency and optical properties of the as-developed photocatalyst were investigated comprehensively. The results show that W–CeO2 completely replicated the multi-scale hierarchical pore structure of the wood template. Compared with cerium oxide prepared by the traditional hydrothermal method (0-CeO2), the specific surface area of W–CeO2 was doubled, and the average pore diameter was reduced by 73% to 5.38 nm. Moreover, W–CeO2 showed the characteristics of anisotropy, circular electron diffraction pattern, reduced crystallinity, and higher charge separation efficiency. W–CeO2 also possessed higher electronic activity, greater active oxygen content, and lower electron-hole recombination efficiency. Methylene blue (MB) dye was used as the target pollutant to test the photocatalytic performance of W–CeO2. The MB degradation test results show that using wood as a template greatly improved the catalytic efficiency of the catalyst. The main free radicals involved in the reaction were determined by adding 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ) and tert-butanol (TBA) for comparative experiments. The results of this work can provide new insights into the construction of bio-inspired materials for promoting the photocatalytic reaction of organic dyes and indicate that W–CeO2 photocatalyst is a promising candidate for wastewater treatment application.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of industry, the pollution of aquatic environment caused by the printing and dyeing industry has increased the shortage of water resources and become an alarming environmental problem to be solved globally [1,2]. Organic dyes, such as methylene blue, are often used to color cotton and silk, but they are detrimental to human beings, aquatic organisms, and safety of water resources. Moreover, they can cause burns to the eyes and even cause breathing difficulties, posing a great threat to human health [3]. Therefore, it is of great practical significance to explore effective methods to treat organic dyes in the discharged effluent from industries. At present, traditional sewage treatment technologies include adsorption processes, membrane filtration, and biological methods [4]. However, these methods have multiple drawbacks such as harmful by-products, low reaction efficiency, complex processing technology, and high costs [5]. Semiconductor-based photocatalysis has attracted increasing attention as a promising method to address the limitations of traditional wastewater treatment, due to its unique features of long-term stability, cost-efficiency, and simple processes [6]. Moreover, it has been reported that active species produced during photocatalysis can effectively degrade many pollutants and toxic compounds [7,8].

At present, metal oxides are the dominant catalysts in photocatalyst research. CeO2, a rare earth oxide, is a typical semiconductor photocatalytic material with a special 4f electron layer structure, which has good application prospects [9,10]. Due to the face-centered cubic structure of cerium oxide, there are a large number of oxygen vacancies in its surface and body. The existence of these oxygen vacancies makes CeO2 suitable for applications in adsorption and catalytic treatment of water pollution [11–14]. Wu et al. prepared CeO2 for the efficient degradation of volatile organic compounds under vacuum ultraviolet light [15]. Ye et al. enhanced the degradation performance of CeO2/SnS2 heterostructure for phenol by introducing nano-carbon layer between CeO2 and SnS2 [16]. As a kind of mesoporous material, the photocatalytic performance of cerium oxide largely depends on its mesoporous structure and volume. The traditional hydrothermal preparation of cerium oxide results in a small mesoporous volume and a large band gap, which limits its application and development.

Wood is a natural polymer based composite material with a clearly ordered structure. Wood cells are composed of primary walls, secondary walls and cavities, and wood cell walls have a multi-scale hierarchical structure when viewed under a microscope [17,18]. Besides the fine multi-scale classification structure, wood also has a hierarchical porous structure formed naturally, and different tree species show different characteristics [19]. In broadleaf tree species, the shape of the pipe hole is varied, and the pipes with larger diameter and smaller diameter form an interphase distribution structure. Conifer wood has a more uniform aperture and more regular distribution [20]. Due to its superior graded-porous structure, low cost, renewable property, and rich variety, wood is considered an ideal template-material. The presence of wood as the template in the synthesis of photocatalyst can endow the photocatalyst with a higher specific surface area than a typical photocatalyst [21]. This is attributed to the fact that the photocatalyst prepared by the wood-template method replicates the multi-scale hierarchical pore structure of the wood template and the abundant pores produced by the inherent property of wood are conducive to the absorption of pollutants.

As mentioned above, the multi-scale classification structure and hierarchical porous structure of wood make it an ideal template for remnant materials. Taking wood as template, cerium oxide can have fine graded porosity like natural wood. This can greatly improve its oxygen storage capacity and band gap characteristics, which would be of great significance for its application in photocatalysis. In this paper, ordinary hydrothermal cerium oxide (0-CeO2) and wood structured cerium oxide (W–CeO2) were prepared, and the influence of wood template on the morphology and mesoporous structure of CeO2 was investigated. The degradation rate of methylene blue was used as an evaluation index, and the photocatalytic performances of W–CeO2 and 0-CeO2 were compared. Moreover, the photocatalytic mechanism of wood cerium oxide was systematically analyzed.

All reagents were of analytical grade and were used as received without further purification. Larch wood splinters were used as the templates of CeO2. The reagents, including Ce(NO3)3·6H2O, ammonia, ethyl alcohol, 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ), tert-butanol (TBA) and methylene blue (MB) were purchased from Tianjin Fuchen chemical reagent factory (China).

The wood pretreatment process was as follows: The larch sheet was cut into small wood pieces, and steamed in 400 mL of 5% dilute ammonia water for 6 h at 85 °C. The wood chips were washed multiple times with deionized water and dehydrated with anhydrous ethanol. The pre-treated wood chips were dried in an oven at 80 °C for 12 h.

CeO2 was prepared by hydrothermal method. First, 2.0 g of Ce(NO3)3·6H2O was dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water, and stirred with a magnetic stirrer until the solid powder was completely dissolved. Then, 5% ammonia was added into the solution dropwise and the solution immediately became turbid. Next, the mixture was transferred to an autoclave, and then reacted at 150 °C for 4 h. After the reactor was cooled to room temperature, the solid-liquid mixture in the reactor was centrifuged and the precipitate was collected. The precipitate was washed with deionized water and ethanol for three times, and then dried at 80 °C. Finally, the dried solid was calcined in a tube furnace at 450 °C for 4 h. The yellowish powder obtained was labeled as 0-CeO2.

The preparation process of the wood structure cerium oxide (W–CeO2) was as follows. First, cerium hydroxide was generated inside the wood by the double-agent diffusion method, and then cerium oxide was obtained by calcination. In a typical method, 4.8 g of cerium hexahydrate was dissolved in 260 mL deionized water and stirred with a magnetic stirrer for 12 h to obtain a completely dispersed precursor solution. The pretreated wood was immersed in the precursor solution for 30 min with ultrasonic oscillation, and then soaked in a vacuum dryer for 4 h. The soaked wood was then dipped in dilute ammonia solution for 4 h. Then, the soaked wood was placed in an oven for 16 h at 90 °C. Finally, the dried wood chips were calcined in a tube furnace at 500 °C for 5 h. After the tube furnace was cooled to room temperature, the pale-yellow wood structure cerium oxide was obtained.

The microstructure and surface morphology of 0-CeO2 and W–CeO2 were characterized by scanning electron microscope (SEM, HITACHI S−520) with a working voltage of 12.5 kV. The surface morphology of samples was characterized using transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100) and the acceleration voltage was set at 200 KV. The crystal structure of 0-CeO2 and W–CeO2 was analyzed by an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, max-2200VPC) at the scanning speed of 5°/min. The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) of the photocatalysts were obtained by a spectrophotometer (TU-1900 UV–VISIBLE) with a scanning wavelength of 200–800 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed by using a THERMO spectrometer. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000) to determine the charge separation behavior and efficiency of the prepared samples.

The degradation of toxic methylene blue (MB) dye in aqueous solution was carried out to test the photocatalytic performance of 0-CeO2 and W–CeO2. In a typical procedure, 0.1 g of W–CeO2/0-CeO2 powder was dispersed in 100 mL MB dye solution at an initial concentration of 10 ppm (1 0 mg/L, UV absorbance of 0.35). Before irradiation, the mixture of MB and catalyst was stirred in the dark for 12 h to achieve adsorption equilibrium on the photocatalyst surface. The photocatalytic activity of 0-CeO2 and W–CeO2 for MB degradation was investigated under visible light using a 380 W xenon lamp equipped with 510 nm cutoff filter. Before and during the test, the MB solution with photocatalyst was dispersed under the condition of constant air balance. During the reaction, the light source was turned on, and sample aliquots were taken once every 0.5 h for a total of 2 h. Then, the collected samples were filtered through a syringe filter with a pore size of 0.22 μm. Finally, UV–vis spectrophotometer was used to measure the absorbance of the sample at 664 nm to evaluate the degradation rate of MB. The degradation efficiency of MB was calculated by using equation (1):
(1) Degradation (%) = [(C
0 – C)/C
0 ] × 100%
where C0 and C represent the initial concentration of MB after the adsorption-desorption equilibrium and the concentration of MB at each sampling time, respectively.

W–CeO2 photocatalyst was used for free radical trapping experiments to determine the contribution of reactive species to the removal of pollutant. 1,4-Benzoquinone (BQ) and tert-butanol (TBA) were employed as scavengers of superoxide radicals (·O2 −) and hydroxyl radicals (·OH), respectively. The concentration of the capture agent was 1 mM.

Fig. 1 presents the SEM photo of W–CeO2. Under SEM, the macroscopic pores (50–1500 μm) of the wood can be observed, which are classified as macropores according to the IUPAC pore definition [22]. As can be seen from the picture, W–CeO2 retained some wood cell morphology. Moreover, it can be seen that the cross section of W–CeO2 was similar to that of larch, and the morphology of axial tracheids, parenchyma cells and other cells can be observed. On the vertical section, numerous fracture marks and the morphology of transverse tracheids, ray cells and axial tracheids in the wood were observed. In addition, the thickening of the cell wall and the thickening of the pit edge were also observed on the longitudinal section. Many small holes were observed on the wall, which may be created by the escape of water vapor from the pits in the wood during calcination [23]. From Fig. 1 b, c, and d, it can be seen that W–CeO2 was actually a thin layer in the microscopic view, indicating that the ceria particles adhered to the cell wall to form a continuous film. Thus, W–CeO2 can replicate the structure and hole characteristics of the wood cell wall.

Fig. 2 shows the TEM picture of 0-CeO2 prepared by hydrothermal method. From the two pictures a and b, it can be found that 0-CeO2 exhibited a cubic shape [24–26]. The cube sample had a regular appearance, and there was no significant agglomeration phenomenon, indicating that the sample had good dispersibility. The surface and edges of the sample were smooth, and the size was concentrated between 20–25 nm. In addition, 0-CeO2 exhibited good crystal characteristics, as evident from the obvious lattice fringes under high-resolution TEM. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern shows that the diffraction pattern of 0-CeO2 was a series of concentric rings, indicating its anisotropy [27,28]. The radius of the ring is related to the type of crystal plane. The square ratio of the reciprocal of the four ring radii in the middle was 3:4:8:11, representing the (111), (200), (220), and (311) lattice planes [29].

Fig. 3 shows the TEM images of W–CeO2. It can be seen from Fig. 3a that although W– CeO2 was ground to powder, part of the morphology of wood was still preserved [30]. The microscopic appearance of W–CeO2 was flaky, indicating that cerium oxide formed a thin film on the wood cell wall and enveloped the wood cells. Due to the gaps between cell walls and ravines on the wood surface, there was some thickening in the W–CeO2 sample. W–CeO2 also had lattice fringes, but no obvious crystal configuration was observed. It can be seen from Fig. 3 f and g that the particle size of the unit cell of W–CeO2 was less than 10 nm, which was much smaller than that of 0-CeO2. Therefore, W–CeO2 exhibited a larger specific surface area. Moreover, the small crystal grain size was conducive to the transfer of photogenerated electrons to the surface of the crystal grain for photocatalytic reaction [31]. The SAED pattern (Fig. 3h) shows that W–CeO2 was also polycrystalline, and the ratio of the ring radius was similar to that of 0-CeO2. Hence, W–CeO2 and 0-CeO2 possessed similar crystal structures [29]. In addition, because the wood template was anisotropic, W–CeO2 also showed anisotropy.

Fig. 4 a shows the XRD spectra of cerium oxide prepared by the two methods. It can be seen that there was almost no difference between the characteristic peaks of W–CeO2 and 0-CeO2, which was consistent with the standard cards of cerium oxide (PDF#34–0394, PDF#43–1002). The diffraction peaks at 28.5°, 33°, 47°, 56°, 59°, 69.4°, 76.7°, and 79° were attributed to (111), (200), (220), (311), (222), (400), (331), and (420) lattice planes of CeO2, respectively [32,33]. However, the peaks of W–CeO2 become somewhat sharper and more intense. The increase in the size of the W–CeO2 crystallites could be ascribed to increased local heating due to the exothermic combustion of the wood template compared with the traditional hydrothermal 0-CeO2 [34,35]. In addition, the individual peaks of W–CeO2 were slightly shifted compared to the standard card and 0-CeO2. This might be due to the existence of macroscopic residual stress between the anisotropic wood template and W–CeO2. Therefore, anisotropic shrinkage occurred during the grain growth process, and W–CeO2 also retained the anisotropy of wood.

In order to study the mesoporous structure of cerium oxide, nitrogen adsorption tests were carried out on the CeO2 samples obtained by hydrothermal method and wood template method (test range 2–100 nm). As seen from Fig. 4b, both 0-CeO2 and W–CeO2 samples demonstrate characteristic type IV adsorption-desorption isotherms according to IUPAC classification, indicating the presence of mesoporous structure in all samples [36]. A H3 type hysteresis loop could be observed in the P/P0 = 0.4 for the W–CeO2, which illustrates that the existence of mesopores. From the shape of hysteresis ring, W–CeO2 begins to close at P/P0 = 0.4, which is a typical mesoporous material, while the hysteresis ring of 0-CeO2 has closed at P/P0 = 0.7, indicating that the mesoporous volume of W–CeO2 is much larger than that of 0-CeO2 [37]. It can also be seen from the pore size distribution curve in Fig. 4c that the pores of W–CeO2 were concentrated at about 3–5 nm and distributed in a concentrated way. However, the peak of 0-CeO2 between 0 and 50 nm was very wide and the pore diameter distribution was not uniform. BJH pore size distributions BET surface areas, average pore size and pore volume of 0-CeO2 and W–CeO2 samples were measured by N2 adsorption analysis and the results are summarized in Table 1 . As shown in Table 1, the specific surface area of W–CeO2 was 64.8 m2/g, almost twice as large as that of 0-CeO2. In contrast, the pore volume of W–CeO2 was much smaller. The average pore diameter was only 5.38 nm, which was far smaller than that of 0-CeO2 (19.82 nm). This may be due to the fact that the particles of W–CeO2 powder were smaller and lighter. Hence, the gap between the particles was smaller, resulting in smaller pore volume.

W–CeO2 and 0-CeO2 nanosamples were further analyzed by XPS technique to clarify their surface chemical composition and chemical states of different elements. The presence of C, O, and Ce components on the surface of the sample was confirmed by XPS measurements, as shown in Fig. 5 a [38]. Fig. 5b and c show the fitting plots of Ce3d and O1s for W–CeO2 and 0-CeO2. The Ce3d spectrum could be fitted into a superposition of eight peaks. The peaks with binding energies around 916.7 eV, 907.3 eV, 901.1 eV, 898.3 eV, 888.6 eV, and 882.4 eV belonged to Ce4+ in the cerium oxide lattice, while the peaks at 903.7 eV and 885.0 eV represented Ce3+ [38–42]. The peaks at binding energy of 529.7 eV represented O2− in the cerium oxide lattice, that is, lattice oxygen; while the peaks at binding energies of 531.8 eV and 533.5 eV corresponded to active oxygen species (O2−, O2 2−) [43–45]. In cerium oxide, Ce4+ and Ce3+ could be converted into each other [46]. During this process, oxygen would overflow and form oxygen vacancies. The conversion equation is as follows: 4Ce4+ + O2− → 4Ce4+ + 2e−/□ + 0.5O2 → 2Ce4+ + 4Ce3+ + □ + 0.5O2 [47]. In the above formula, the symbol □ represents oxygen holes. It can be found that the content of trivalent cerium atoms in cerium oxide was closely related to the content of active oxygen. Table 2 shows the contents of Ce3+ and active oxygen. It can be seen that both Ce3+ and active oxygen contents of W–CeO2 were slightly increased, indicating that cerium oxide prepared by the wood template method had more surface defects and more active oxygen free radicals.

Fig. 6 a presents the UV diffuse reflectance spectra of the two forms of cerium oxide. It can be seen that the absorption behavior of W–CeO2 at 200–300 nm was the same as that of 0-CeO2. Both samples had a wider peak from 200 nm to 300 nm. The reflection intensity of W–CeO2 was lower than that of 0-CeO2, indicating that W–CeO2 absorbed visible light more efficiently [48]. In addition, W–CeO2 had one more peak at 335 nm compared to 0-CeO2, possibly because W–CeO2 had more surface defects. The Kubelka-Munk function was implemented to assess the absorbance property. Fig. 6b shows the Tauc-plot curves of 0-CeO2 and W–CeO2.The band gap energy of 0-CeO2 and W–CeO2 was depicted by means of the Tauc plot according to equation (2):
(2)
F(R∞)hν = A(hν - E
g )n/2
where F(R∞), h, ν, A, and E
g represent Kubelka-Munk function, planck constant, vibration frequency (Hz), a proportional constant, and band gap energy (eV), respectively [49,50]. The value of index n lies on the property of material, which is 1 for a direct transition semiconductor and 4 for an indirect transition semiconductor [4]. According to the linear portion of the [F(R∞)hν]2 - (hν) curves, the E g of 0-CeO2 was determined to be 3.6 eV, which was very close to the 3.5 eV reported in the literature [36]. The E g of W–CeO2 reduced to 3.3 eV, indicating that the wood template method significantly adjusted the band gap of cerium oxide, which resulted in a lower electron-hole recombination rate. Therefore, the photocatalytic performance of W–CeO2 was improved.

The catalytic performance of photocatalysts was analyzed via the degradation of MB. In order to evaluate the photocatalytic activity of the samples, MB (a typical dye that is widely used in aquatic environments) was selected as the target degradation product. The degradation curves of the two samples are shown in Fig. 7 a. Each photocatalytic reaction was carried out after the adsorption equilibrium was reached within 60 min in darkness. W–CeO2 possessed higher adsorption capacity, mainly because of its higher oxygen vacancy and specific surface area. After the adsorption equilibrium was established, the photocatalytic reaction was carried out. It was found that the degradation rate of MB gradually increased with the passage of time. After 2 h of photocatalysis, the degradation rate of MB by W–CeO2 reached nearly 90%. At the same time, the degradation rate of MB by 0-CeO2 was only about 40%, indicating that the photocatalytic activity of W–CeO2 was far greater than that of 0-CeO2.

It is well known that the recombination of photogenerated carriers leads to PL emission [51]. Thus, PL spectroscopy was used to reveal the charge separation and transfer efficiency of electron-hole pairs in W–CeO2 and 0-CeO2 samples by comparing fluorescence intensities. PL emission spectra were recorded at room temperature with an excitation wavelength of 320 nm, and all the prepared samples showed emission peaks near 450 nm. The PL spectra of W–CeO2 sample and 0-CeO2 sample are shown in Fig. 7b. The lowest emission intensity was found in W–CeO2, which means that photogenerated e−-h+ pairs were more easily separated and migrated in W–CeO2. Undoubtedly, W–CeO2 showed reproducible photocurrent response as well as a higher photocurrent response than 0-CeO2. This indicates that the photoinduced e−-h+ pair was slower and the carrier mobility was higher. This result is consistent with its high photocatalytic activity.

In order to determine the stability and persistence of W–CeO2, a photocatalytic cycle experiment was carried out, as shown in Fig. 7c. After four cycles of testing, W–CeO2 showed no significant loss of activity, confirming that W–CeO2 had good stability.

To determine the main species involved in the photodegradation reaction, tert-butanol (TBA) and benzoquinone (BQ) were added into the system as capture agents. BQ is an inhibitor of superoxide radical, and TBA is an inhibitor of hydroxyl radical. Fig. 7d shows that only a small amount of MB was degraded in the reaction system when TBA was added, while the degradation rate of MB in the reaction system with BQ was slightly reduced. Therefore, it can be determined that the main species involved in the reaction was hydroxyl radical. Accordingly, the reaction mechanism of MB degradation by W–CeO2 can be speculated.

In summary, W–CeO2 demonstrated improved photocatalytic performance under visible light irradiation. Due to its larger specific surface area and pore volume, W–CeO2 can absorb more oxygen and MB, which promotes the generation of oxygen-containing free radicals for subsequent reaction [52]. In addition, due to the presence of more oxygen holes, the band gap of W–CeO2 is narrower, indicating that the electrons inside the crystal have smaller binding energy. Consequently, the electrons can easily absorb light energy and transition to the conduction band, thus generating more electron hole pairs [53,54]. W–CeO2 can be driven by natural light to produce carriers (Equation (3)). Subsequently, electrons (e−) in valence band (VB) rapidly transfer to conduction band (CB), while relatively stable holes (h+) remain in VB. Due to the narrower cell radius of W–CeO2, the path of photogenerated electron migration to the grain surface is shorter, and the probability of photogenerated electron hole recombination during migration is also smaller. This is more conducive to the generation of hydroxyl radical [55]. These electrons produce reactive ·O2 − radicals by reducing O2 (Equation (4)). In addition, the holes on VB oxidize H2O and OH- to produce ·OH radicals (Equation (5) and (6)). Finally, a large number of photoactive substances (· O2 − and ·OH) participate in the efficient degradation of MB. Therefore, compared with 0-CeO2, the catalytic efficiency of W–CeO2 was improved. Based on the above discussion, the schematic diagram of photocatalytic mechanism of W–CeO2 is shown in Fig. 8 .
(3) CeO2 + hv → CeO2 (e− + h+)
(4) O2 + e− → ·O2 −
(5) h+ + H2O → ·OH + H+
(6) h+ + OH− →·OH
(7) MB+·OH/·O2 − → CO2+H2O

In this paper, cerium oxide with wood structure (W–CeO2) was prepared by using larch wood as a template. Its morphology and structure were characterized, and the mechanism of enhanced photocatalytic degradation performance was explored. The prepared W–CeO2 closely replicated the multi-scale and multi-dimensional pore structure of the wood template. Hence, the sample exhibited a larger specific surface area and better adsorption ability for oxygen and MB. At the same time, W–CeO2 also had more oxygen holes than 0-CeO2, which can form more oxygen-containing free radicals. The band gap of W–CeO2 was about 0.3 eV smaller than that of 0-CeO2, indicating that electrons can be more easily excited to the conduction band. Compared to 0-CeO2, W–CeO2 had a smaller and more uniform crystal size, which helped the transfer of photogenerated electrons. In summary, the efficiency of photocatalytic degradation of MB was considerably improved by using the wood structured W–CeO2 photocatalyst.

Zhuoying Cheng: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft. Suyue Luo: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft. Zhao Liu: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Visualization. Ying Zhang: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Visualization. Yushuang Liao: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Visualization. Minghui Guo: Resources, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Tat Thang Nguyen: Resources, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31971587) and the Special Project for Double First−Class−Cultivation of Innovative Talents (000/41113102).
